speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four-- this is tape number four of the interview with Jacob Rosenberg. Jacob, until when did you remain in the Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be August, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in August?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the settlement, the liquidation, of the ghetto. We were caught in the street. My family were caught in the street by Germans and transported to-- to the place of-- off to the station, which was sort of organized from the people who went to Auschwitz. They didn't caught me but I-- I volunteered. I didn't want my parents to go without me. So I volunteered then. And after two days, I think-- I can't remember exactly-- two days or a day and a half, yeah, I arrived to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, cattle train, we were about 100 people. In one-- in one wagon, or cart, you might call it. They provided with a-- with a bucket of water and a bucket for to relieve ourselves. Within about a few hours, the bucket of water was empty, and the bucket of-- of sediment was full.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any food on your journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no food. We had a little bit of food which was given to us, and we economized. And we-- my mother was-- we were so foolish. My mother was sort of thinking that she hasn't-- because we were caught on the street, that she hasn't gotten any dresses to change. My father had no idea what she thought. We're going somewhere to another working place. Didn't realize what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody take anything with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing, nothing, we-- we were just without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you taking, like, straight from the street to the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the street, they were caught in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that we're going to Auschwitz. But my parents wouldn't believe it. And my-- my sisters, nobody believed it, what was going to happen. I knew this was going to-- taking place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were notified by the Polish radio [NON-ENGLISH]. And we caught a meeting in the morning by a young-- young, the young movement, [NON-ENGLISH] movement, if we're going to-- to make a stand against them. And I remember where the meeting took place. It took place in-- school friends of mine, her name [NON-ENGLISH], in her house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went up. And I said-- I was on the stairs going to the meeting. I met a friend of mine going down. And his name was [?Hanner Koffman?] And he said, we decided not to make a stand because we haven't given any weapons, and to try to hide and do the best we can to-- to oppose the-- the resettlement. That was that. How long did you travel by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you I can't remember exactly. It was-- it was, I think, a day and a half, or maybe two days. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stopped-- it stopped, I think, in [NON-ENGLISH] we stopped. And we ask a group of people who stood there, our compatriots. They knew where we were going, and we asked them if they know where we are going. And they said, one of them said, that you're going there where you belong.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived at Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when the train arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was organized by the Germans. They knew how to create a chaos and panic. And-- and the doors were open. You know, there were sealed doors. They were open. Raus, raus, raus-- and they had the dogs. And we-- everybody was petrified. It was terrible. We didn't know what's-- people were jumping. Other people were jumping from the trains, broken their limbs, and little kids. And before you knew, this-- this-- this was to terrorize, you know, to not give the people a chance to-- to think what's taking place. It was all premeditated, and also this was a plan. I-- I called it-- they-- they are planning chaos. They planned chaos. That's what they did, and it worked. And then they lined up men separate and women separate. And everybody had to lift up their hand like this. Over. And they have to mention only your age and how old you are. And according to this, we were given-- it was life or death. If you're over the limit, so, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the limit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I describe it again in my poem, Of which this is the best-- the best sort of, to me, perhaps. 'On the thirteenth date of the month, after a journey of tears and sweat, my family and I arrived to Germany's kingdom of death. I remember our last embrace, mother breaking down. What will happen to the children? Will I ever see you, my son? Oh, I know what happened to the children. I know what became of them all. But to keep what's left of my sanity, there are scenes I refuse to recall. The scene is in stony bunker, the last plea for breath, the smell of gas, the eerie silence, dead. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] But in the night, day, where the pit is still aflame, I hear them calling my name-- my name, my shame. I once had a mother and father, two sisters in their prime of life-- Ida the gentle dove, and Pola, [INAUDIBLE]. I also had two nieces, little girls of six and four, one with a windowless basement, the other with a lock on the door. Now, they are only ashes and an everlasting sigh. And in the twilight house, a mother's cry.' That's how it was. There's no exaggerations here. Since the last scenes in Auschwitz-- Auschwitz, and Birkenau, when I met-- when I was-- was with my parents, I perfectly depict here. And like I said before, the metaphor is under the magnifying glass. There's nothing hiding behind it. It's the exposure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your whole family with you when you arrived in Auschwitz? Who was there, your mother, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my father, my two sisters, my two little nieces, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you put your hands up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody made a selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it's Mengele. He stood. He made a selection. A pair of white gloves, with a riding stick I think he had, or some other sort of. And he pointed at left, right, left, right, left, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which way did he point for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: For your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister right, the other left, and the two nieces left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister went right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pola, so she went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not with me, because there was men-- men and women separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people who went left?

speaker: interviewee
text: There all were gassed, all died the same day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were-- they were taken into-- you know, the economy of murder took place. Germany wouldn't-- they didn't lost anything. They made-- from everything, they made use. They got their clothing, their hair, they gold teeth, their shoes, their skins, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You get-- you got into, in the morning, through the gate, and by-- by dusk, you went out into-- to the chimney in smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that that was what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't think so that any-- any person can-- can imagine that something like this is taking place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think had happened to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. But the day-- the day afterwards, we knew because the old inmates told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how your mother looked the last time you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to recall. I-- I-- I recall, I constantly go back to ghetto. And I understand why most of my friends do that. They constantly talk about the past. Although this past was hell, yet in that hell, in the dream-- in the-- in the tragedy of hell, there is a-- a sense of nostalgia. Because their parents were there, and their families were there, and there was a home. I'm talking about ghetto. I'm not talking about camp here. And therefore, you can't find people-- they're recalling that time with a certain sense of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of warmth. Funny enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see what happened to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I discovered my sister later on in camp. That took me for-- for work, some useless work, you know, to carry stones from one place. One morning, we carried it from-- from left to right. And the next morning, they took us out to carry from right to left. Useless work, but just to-- sort of to-- to dehumanize us. And all of a sudden, I-- I passed by the-- the camp where they-- they kept the women. And there was my sister. She starts screaming, Jacob, Jacob, my name. And she was there. So I told her I'd volunteer for this work tomorrow. I'd be able to talk to her. And I did. And this went like this a day or two. But obviously, she learned that they gassed her little child. And in the night, she walked out from the barrack and hung herself on the electric wires. And when I came the following morning, she was still hanging on the wire in a white shirt, a-- a nightgown. Not in the sense like we know a nightgown now, but something to cover her body with her shaven head. I actually have a painting which I made of her. It-- it became sort of a-- I had to get it out of me. So I have a painting. Later on, I can show you what the-- what the scene looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can go back just a little bit to after the selection, what happened immediately after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken to shaved, bathed, and taken to a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, there was nothing. We just waited to be murdered. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nothing else you could do. If they were short-- [BACKROUND NOISE] Somebody knocks at the door. If they were short--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are resuming taping after a break. Jacob, were you given a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't got a number. The Germans had a system. Those that were given numbers were registered as working possibilities. And those without numbers were so-called stock, in case they run out of supplies to murder. They took from all those barracks where the-- the inmates had no numbers. And I belonged to this sort of group, to that stock. So any day-- any day if they were short, they could have taken me to-- to become smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister have a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that I know of. I-- I-- I-- there's no way I could know. But I assume no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you had much opportunity to speak to her--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we just had hello. I-- she called my name. I called back her name. That's all that we could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you realize that it was your sister who was hanging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon as I seen her, I know. I knew her. It was my sister. Of course I could recognize my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing, just broke down, and that's it. Went down. One of the essential things to survive was not to think. Thinking was devastating.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you stop yourself from thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your day to day life in Auschwitz, your daily routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no routine. We got up. By the way, they woke us up about 5 o'clock in the morning. We had to line up for coffee, so-called coffee. And then they lined us up again till about-- we stood up from six, let's say, till about nine. And they kept counting us. Counting was one of the most wonderful pastimes for Germans, to count us, to make sure that we all there. They kept counting us. Then they made mistakes. And obviously, they made-- they didn't make mistakes because they couldn't count. They made it purposely to keep us constantly on our feet. And you could not fall. You could not sit down. You could not do anything. You got to stand there. If-- if you-- if you fall, you were the end of you, if you fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the people who had no numbers do in the time of counting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, the same thing. They were counted as well as those who had numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the counting was over, then what would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We dispersed. We had to line up again for-- for a little bit of soup. And then we-- we had to line-- we had to line up again. The whole day was taken with counting. By 5 o'clock, we had to be back in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard to say, I think we were about-- about 400. There was room for 200, was about.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the room look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no room. It was just a barrack with bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people slept on a bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this-- in-- in Birkenau in that-- in Birkenau, there was no bunks. They weren't-- I got confused a bit. The camps had bunks. In Birkenau, there was no bunks. We slept on the-- on the cement floor. And we stood up one next to the other. We were lined up in 10s. And we had to sit down, and we each of us kept another one between his legs. And that's the way we slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to eat during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a great, a little bit of soup and perhaps 100 gram of bread in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did your health-- how did you remain healthy in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I stood barefoot in snow without clothes. I never got a cold, never-- never got sick. Now, I go out, and-- and it's a little bit-- it's getting cool or a wind, I get a cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any special friends, any close friends, in the-- in the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one friend. And I also befriended a Dutch boy whose parents-- his father was an editor of a social democratic paper in [NON-ENGLISH]. And as the Germans entered Poland, they-- he was beheaded with his wife. And he was sent to camp. He was about 40 years old. How he lasted so many years, I wouldn't have a clue. He-- when I was there, he was already 2 and 1/2 years in Auschwitz. And I became friendly with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend the whole time you were in Auschwitz carrying rocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What clothing did you wear--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a uniform. You know that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: --black and white striped uniforms, paper, or whatever it was. I don't know. They made it from sort of synthetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of punishments were people given in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: All sorts of. Depends on what-- what the-- the so-called crime was. For stealing a piece of bread, you used to get about 25 lashes on you. Oh, this is stealing bread from-- from a communal source. But if you stole the bread from-- from another inmate, they hung you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see many people being hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've seen about, I'd say about, five or six hanging. Not in Auschwitz just, but in [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto. In the beginning of the war, I've seen hanging three people. In Auschwitz, I've seen hanging. And I've also seen hanging in Ebensee, my last camp, where I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see somebody you knew being hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort orders were you given during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: At Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz, not-- there was no orders. Look, this-- we were not working there. We were just in sort of a transit between dead and work. They couldn't make up their mind. Or this was most probably 1944, and there-- there was a great shortage in German of-- labor shortage. And they kept us like, you know, like slaves. And from time to time, a German entrepreneur came and selected a few people for his-- to dig trenches and to-- to-- attend to-- railway lines-- lines, sort of. And I was-- I was picked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- to Upper Silesia. It's still Germany, I think, in one of the camps. It belonged-- it was a sister camp of Gross Rosen. And then from there, I-- I was transported to-- a camp by the name of Oberwustegiersdorf. I was in-- included in a-- in a team that worked for a firm-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I worked in that camp for about three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if we can just go back. How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, you went to Upper Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I spent about three months in those camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel to Upper Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- by train, by cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the crematoriums in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of any sabotage of the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was sabotage, but I was not connected with it. And it was done by a very sort of esoteric group, the group, clandestine group, which had no communication with the general population in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Auschwitz, was-- was the crematorium still operational?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you travel to Upper Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, in the cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us about-- I think about 10 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, we were taken and quarantined on top of a hill. And we spend in this quarantine about seven days. Then they took us down to the major camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a camp which we-- we were absorbed in building a roads. And I was included in a transport team. Transport means carrying things. It doesn't mean to-- that I was involved in-- in locomotion, just carrying things, you know, transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you carry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cement blocks, trees, steel lines, all sorts of commodities, I mean, that were needed for building roads.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a number, but not tattooed. I had a number painted here on my leg. It was 126,139.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it painted?

speaker: interviewee
text: With ink.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same uniform, yeah, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where-- on the journey?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the journey, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a piece of bread and not enough food. You also have to-- you know, if you say food, some people might-- people of-- of our world, think food in a-- it's a different concept. Food is a different concept than food in-- in camp. They gave us enough to-- not to die, but not to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you to eat in Upper Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Upper Silesia, we had the same thing as in Auschwitz but a little bit better. There was a few more potatoes in the soup, and you know, after all, we were a working team. And they-- they didn't want to lose the-- the labor force. So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of working conditions, what sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no conditions. We just were driven out to-- to the place of work. And there, we-- we started to work under the supervision of-- of kapos and-- of German staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time would you start working?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started about I think 7:00 in the morning. We worked till about till-- till the sunset, till there was no more light in the day. There was no specific hours-- specific hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any women in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how many men?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this particular camp, I think it was about 1,000 people. It was a small camp, maybe 1,500.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what things did they die from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hunger, starvation, exhaustion, beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went, once the Russians start moving, and-- on the front line of central Europe, they took us from there. And I was taken to a camp Ebensee in the mountains, Austrian mountains, in [NON-ENGLISH]. This was probably the horriblest camp I ever been to. There was never-- I couldn't see the light. We were in a-- in a forest. I can't remember seeing the sky there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was blocking the sky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge, perhaps, primordial trees, so big giants. Now--
